Control Update Build

Build Your Own MIM

Mouse Input Modifier · DIY Guide · English

MIM is open hardware. Anyone can build their own device using the same firmware that runs in the finished Alpha units. This guide covers three build tiers that build on each other — followed by firmware flashing instructions that apply to all tiers equally.

Build Tiers Overview

TierNameSolderingWhat you get
Tier 1 Bare Bones ~25 min, basic skills Fully functional, no enclosure
Tier 2 Compact Build ~40 min Permanently soldered, reset button, stable
Tier 3 Full Build ~2 h 2× TFT, 2× encoder, aluminium enclosure

Tier 1 — Bare Bones #

Goal: A working MIM device as fast as possible, minimal soldering. Ideal for trying things out before investing more time.

Parts

PartSourceNote
Teensy 4.1SparkFun / DigiKeySparkFun DEV-20360
Micro-USB cableanyTeensy → PC
USB-A socket on breakout boardAliExpress / Amazonfor mouse / keyboard / controller
4× jumper wires (male–female)any10–15 cm is enough
Soldering iron + solder—Basic equipment

USB Host Pin Assignment

The Teensy 4.1 has five pads on its bottom side for the USB host connection. This is where you connect the mouse, keyboard, or controller.

Pad 2  →  +5V   (USB Pin 1)
Pad 3  →  D−    (USB Pin 2)
Pad 4  →  D+    (USB Pin 3)
Pad 5  →  GND   (USB Pin 4)
Pad 6  →  GND   (optional, can share with Pad 5)
PHOTO: Teensy 4.1 bottom side — all five host pads visible and labelled. Ideally with wires already soldered, colours clearly visible (e.g. red=+5V, white=D−, green=D+, black=GND). High-contrast background, good lighting. [T1-A]

Schematic

MOUSE / KEYBOARD / CONTROLLER
        │
  USB-A Socket (Breakout)
  Pin 1 (+5V)  ──── Teensy bottom Pad 2
  Pin 2 (D−)   ──── Teensy bottom Pad 3
  Pin 3 (D+)   ──── Teensy bottom Pad 4
  Pin 4 (GND)  ──── Teensy bottom Pad 5
        │
     [Teensy 4.1]
        │
  Micro-USB (top)
        │
       PC
Wiring diagram image not available (firmware repo may be private).

MIM Wiring Diagram v03 — Overview

PHOTO: Finished Bare Bones build from above — Teensy with soldered USB-A socket, Micro-USB cable towards PC, mouse already connected. Wiring must be recognisable, no loose cables. [T1-B]

Tier 2 — Compact Build #

Builds on Tier 1. Permanently soldered on perfboard, with reset button and buffer capacitors. This setup runs stably even with high-resolution gaming mice (Beast X Pro, 4K polling).

Additional Parts vs. Tier 1

PartValue / TypeSourceNote
Perfboard~80 × 50 mmAliExpress / ConradTeensy fits on 56-pin board
Buffer capacitor 11000 µF / 16 V, Low-ESRConrad / ReicheltElectrolytic, on USB host VBUS/GND
Buffer capacitor 2100 nF, MLCCConrad / ReicheltCeramic, parallel to electrolytic
Reset buttonMomentary, illuminatedAliExpresse.g. RUNCCI-YUN 16 mm
USB host cableUSB-A socket with pigtailAliExpressinstead of breakout board
Wire4× 0.25 mm²anyfor USB host wiring

Why Capacitors?

High-resolution mice (2000Hz and more) can cause brief voltage drops on the VBUS line during enumeration, leading to connection drops. The capacitors buffer these spikes directly at the Teensy's host port.

Placement: both capacitors in parallel between Pad 2 (+5V) and Pad 5 (GND) on the bottom of the Teensy, as close to the pads as possible.

PHOTO: Teensy bottom side with both capacitors soldered — electrolytic and MLCC visibly wired in parallel on the host pads. Electrolytic polarity recognisable. [T2-A]

Reset Button

The reset button provides direct access to the HalfKay bootloader — useful when the firmware has locked up or for a manual firmware update.

Connection: Program pad (top left on Teensy, labelled) → GND

[Button] ──── Program pad (Teensy front side, top left)
[Button] ──── GND
A brief press → Teensy boots into the HalfKay bootloader. Teensy Loader recognises it automatically and waits for a new firmware image.
PHOTO: Teensy front side, Program pad marked. Finished wired button, cable to Program pad and GND visible. [T2-B / T2-C]
PHOTO: Complete build on perfboard — Teensy, both capacitors, USB-A socket permanently soldered, button cable leaving. Neat, clear wiring. [T2-D]

Assembly Order

  1. Place Teensy on perfboard, solder pin headers
  2. Mount USB-A socket with pigtail at the edge of the perfboard
  3. Solder capacitors to the bottom of the Teensy (watch electrolytic polarity!)
  4. Solder USB-A cable to host pads (pin assignment as Tier 1)
  5. Wire reset button (Program → GND)
  6. Double-check all connections before first power-on

Tier 3 — Full Build #

Builds on Tier 2. 2× TFT display, 2× encoder, aluminium enclosure, USB-C socket on the panel. Corresponds to the Alpha Edition build.

Additional Parts vs. Tier 2

PartTypeSourceNote
TFT Display 1+2ST7789, 240×280, SPIAliExpress2 pieces
Encoder 1+2KY-040 with buttonAliExpress2 pieces
EnclosureHammond 1455KGYBKMouser / RS Components120 × 65 × 27 mm
End panelsABS, matching Hammond 1455Hammond / Mouseror 3D print
USB-C Panel-MountCF-V8RI, 90°, 10 cmAliExpressMicro-B Male → USB-C Female
Screws / standoffsM2.5, assortmentAmazonfor perfboard mounting inside enclosure

Encoder Assignment

E1 (left)   — Navigation (select parameter)
E2 (right)  — Change value / mode toggle (LongPress)

Both encoders: CLK, DT, SW pins each to free digital pins on the Teensy. Exact pin assignment is in firmware/common/config.h.

TFT Displays (SPI)

Both displays share the SPI bus (MOSI, SCLK, common) but each has its own CS and DC pin.

Display 1: CS → Pin X, DC → Pin Y
Display 2: CS → Pin A, DC → Pin B
The exact pin numbers are defined in config.h and can be adapted to your own wiring if needed.
PHOTO: Teensy with both TFT displays wired, still outside enclosure — SPI bus wiring visible, both displays active. [T3-A]
PHOTO: Hammond enclosure with drilled holes — USB-C panel-mount cutout, reset button hole, encoder holes in front panel. [T3-B]
PHOTO: Interior view fully assembled — perfboard in enclosure slots, cable routing, TFTs mounted on end panels or front panel. [T3-C]
PHOTO: Finished exterior — screwed enclosure, USB-C socket visible, encoders and reset button on front panel. [T3-D]

Initial Firmware Flash

The same firmware runs on all three hardware tiers — no per-tier configuration needed.

  1. Download Teensy Loader (pjrc.com) and install it
  2. Go to mim-control.com/dev-options and download the latest MIM Firmware A .hex file
  3. Open the .hex in Teensy Loader, connect the Teensy via Micro-USB, press the white button on the Teensy — it flashes automatically
PHOTO: Teensy Loader with loaded .hex, progress bar during flash. [T1-C]

Test the Connection

  1. Open a browser (Chrome or Edge — Firefox does not work)
  2. Go to mim-control.com
  3. Click Connect — device should appear
  4. Connect mouse — MIM tab becomes active
PHOTO: mim-control.com in browser, connection established, MIM tab active. [T1-D]

Firmware Update

After the initial flash, updates can be installed directly from the browser.

  1. Go to mim-control.com/update
  2. Current vs. available version is shown
  3. "Download firmware" → .hex file is loaded
  4. Press the white button on the Teensy briefly → boots into bootloader
  5. Teensy Loader detects the bootloader and flashes automatically
Tier 2 and Tier 3 have a dedicated reset button — use it instead of the white button on the Teensy board.

AIM / CIM — XInput Mode

AIM converts mouse input into joystick deflection (XInput output). CIM passes a connected controller through with correction applied. Both modes output XInput signals instead of standard HID.

These modes require Firmware C — a separate firmware image available on mim-control.com/dev-options under "Firmware C". Flash it the same way as Firmware A using Teensy Loader.

Note: mim-control.com does not work with Firmware C. Configuration requires external tools. To return to MIM / KIM, simply flash Firmware A again.

Help & Community

Questions, problems, preset sharing — all in the Discord:

→ Join Discord

The full firmware and this document are public:

→ github.com/boa-constrictor-42/mim-mouse-input-modifier

Photo Checklist for the Build

This list is for the author — all photos needed for this guide.

Tier 1

  • T1-A Teensy 4.1 bottom side — host pads labelled / wires soldered
  • T1-B Finished Bare Bones build from above (Teensy + USB-A socket + Micro-USB cable)
  • T1-C Teensy Loader with loaded .hex, progress bar during flash
  • T1-D mim-control.com in browser, connection established, MIM tab active (screenshot ok)

Tier 2

  • T2-A Capacitors soldered to Teensy bottom (electrolytic + MLCC visible)
  • T2-B Program pad of the Teensy (front side, close-up, marked)
  • T2-C Reset button wired (cable visible)
  • T2-D Complete build on perfboard (overview)

Tier 3

  • T3-A Both TFTs wired, outside enclosure, active
  • T3-B Hammond enclosure with holes (before assembly)
  • T3-C Interior view fully assembled
  • T3-D Finished exterior
